column1: Ishango Bone, Congo Basin, c. 20,000 BCE
column2: Chamber of Number: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: Fishers by Lake Edward, in today's Democratic Republic of the Congo, carved rows of notches into a baboon fibula over 20,000 years ago. Belgian geologist Jean de Heinzelin found it at Ishango in 1950. Many historians of mathematics treat it as one of humanity's oldest counting artifacts, while others urge caution about reading modern arithmetic into ancient marks.
column5:
KQ: How can anyone know what a mark on a bone meant to the hand which carved it?

Suggested answer 1: Patterned groupings invite a mathematical reading. The notches cluster in ways some scholars read as tallies or a lunar count, so the pattern itself serves as evidence of intent.

Suggested answer 2: Intent may stay out of reach. Other scholars read the notches as grip aids, so the same marks support rival explanations and certainty stays low.
column6: "Ishango Bone." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ishango_bone.
column7: Claim card 1: Patterned groupings invite a mathematical reading. | Claim card 2: Intent may stay out of reach. | Fact card: Fishers by Lake Edward, in today's Democratic Republic of the Congo, carved rows of notches into a baboon fibula over 20,000 years ago. | Materials: 2 Bone, 1 Resin

column1: Rømer's Meridian Circle, Copenhagen, 1704
column2: Chamber of Evidence: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: Danish astronomer Ole Rømer, famous for first measuring the speed of light in 1676, built a meridian circle in Copenhagen to time stars crossing the north-south line. The design became a standard tool of positional astronomy for two centuries. Observatories set national clocks by such crossings, tying everyday time to the sky.
column5:
KQ: How far do instruments decide which facts become visible at all?

Suggested answer 1: An instrument opens a channel and narrows it at once. Rømer's circle tracked star crossings with new precision, so time itself became a measurable object rather than a felt flow.

Suggested answer 2: The observer never fully leaves. Rømer still judged each crossing by eye and ear, so the device sharpened human judgment without replacing it.
column6: "Ole Rømer." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ole_R%C3%B8mer.

column1: Hokusai's Woodblocks for the Great Wave, Edo, 1831
column2: Chamber of Meaning: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: Katsushika Hokusai designed Under the Wave off Kanagawa around 1831 as part of his Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. Carvers cut his design into cherry woodblocks, and printers pulled thousands of impressions, which sold cheaply in Edo. The image now hangs in museums worldwide as separate prints of the same blocks, complicating every simple idea of an original.
column5:
KQ: When thousands of prints leave one set of blocks, where does the original live?

Suggested answer 1: The blocks hold the design, so a case exists for calling them the original. Every print is an impression of their carved surface, and the carver's hand set every line.

Suggested answer 2: Originality can live in the design rather than the object. Hokusai drew the image, carvers cut it, printers inked it, so the print is a team's answer and no single object owns it.
column6: "The Great Wave off Kanagawa." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Great_Wave_off_Kanagawa.
column7: Claim card 1: The blocks hold the design, so a case exists for calling them the original. | Claim card 2: Originality can live in the design rather than the object. | Fact card: Katsushika Hokusai designed Under the Wave off Kanagawa around 1831 as part of his Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. | Materials: 2 Pigment, 1 Charcoal

column1: Champollion's Decipherment Notebook, Paris, 1822
column2: Chamber of Memory: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: Jean-François Champollion announced the decipherment of Egyptian hieroglyphs in 1822, after years of notebook pages comparing the Rosetta Stone's three scripts with Coptic. His pages show crossed-out attempts, letter grids, and cartouche sketches. The notebooks turned a dead script into a readable archive of three thousand years.
column5:
KQ: What separates a decipherment from a guess which happened to hold?

Suggested answer 1: Convergence separates them. Champollion's readings predicted names in cartouches he had never seen, and the predictions kept landing, so the system justified itself beyond any single lucky match.

Suggested answer 2: Method separates them. He cross-checked Coptic, Greek, and demotic versions letter by letter, so each claim carried a public chain of reasons a rival could attack and test.
column6: "Jean-François Champollion." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Champollion.
column7: Claim card 1: Convergence separates them. | Claim card 2: Method separates them. | Fact card: Jean-François Champollion announced the decipherment of Egyptian hieroglyphs in 1822, after years of notebook pages comparing the Rosetta Stone's… | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Resin

column1: Milgram's Shock Generator Panel, Yale, 1961
column2: Chamber of the Living: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: Stanley Milgram's obedience experiments at Yale used a fake shock generator with switches labeled up to 450 volts. Volunteers, urged on by a calm experimenter, believed they were shocking a learner in the next room. The results reshaped social psychology and helped create the consent rules and review boards which now govern research on people.
column5:
KQ: Can knowledge gained through deception ever justify the method which produced it?

Suggested answer 1: The findings changed how institutions guard against blind obedience, so some argue the insight repaid its cost, since no transparent design could have revealed the same behavior.

Suggested answer 2: Participants left believing they might have harmed a stranger, so others argue the study purchased knowledge with borrowed consciences, and modern ethics boards now refuse the price.
column6: "Milgram Experiment." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milgram_experiment.

column1: Voyager Golden Record, Master Copy, 1977
column2: Chamber of Themes: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: NASA launched two gold-plated copper records aboard the Voyager probes in 1977, carrying greetings, music, and encoded images chosen by a committee led by Carl Sagan. The records now travel beyond the solar system, the farthest human-made objects. Every debate about the playlist doubles as a debate about who speaks for the species.
column5:
KQ: Who gets to summarize humanity, and by what right?

Suggested answer 1: A small committee chose the sounds and images, so the record shows one team's 1977 answer, complete with its omissions, and naming the authors keeps the summary useful.

Suggested answer 2: Any summary beats silence across interstellar distance. The committee chose greetings in 55 languages and music from many continents, so breadth served as its own defense.
column6: "Voyager Golden Record." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voyager_Golden_Record.

column1: Ramanujan's Lost Notebook, Rediscovered 1976
column2: The Uncounted Room: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: Srinivasa Ramanujan filled loose pages with formulas in his final year, 1919 to 1920, mostly without proofs. George Andrews found the sheets in a Cambridge library box in 1976, and mathematicians have mined them since, proving entry after entry. The pages keep raising the question of how one mind reached results the field needed a century to justify.
column5:
KQ: Do results discovered without proof count as knowledge before the proofs arrive?

Suggested answer 1: Mathematicians spent decades proving the entries true one by one, so the notebook acted as knowledge on credit, trusted because its author's record justified the loan.

Suggested answer 2: A claim without a proof stays a conjecture, whoever wrote it. On this stricter view the notebook held brilliant guesses which became knowledge only as each proof landed.
column6: "Ramanujan's Lost Notebook." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramanujan%27s_lost_notebook.

column1: Le Grand K, International Prototype Kilogram, Sèvres, 1889
column2: The Unmeasured Room: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: From 1889 to 2019, the International Prototype Kilogram, a platinum-iridium cylinder kept at Sèvres near Paris, defined the kilogram for the world. Copies traveled to national labs, and periodic comparisons showed tiny drifts nobody could explain away. The 2019 redefinition tied mass to a fixed constant of physics, ending the reign of the last artifact-based SI unit.
column5:
KQ: What does it mean for a measurement to be objective when its standard is one object in one vault?

Suggested answer 1: For 130 years a kilogram meant equality with one platinum cylinder near Paris, so objectivity rested on shared access to a single thing, guarded under three glass bells.

Suggested answer 2: The cylinder itself drifted by micrograms against its own copies, so in 2019 metrologists redefined the kilogram through Planck's constant, trading a guarded object for a law of nature anyone can measure.
column6: "International Prototype of the Kilogram." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Prototype_of_the_Kilogram.

column1: The Flémalle Panels, Unsigned, c. 1430
column2: The Unsigned Room: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: Three panels in Frankfurt, painted around 1430 in the early Netherlandish style, carry no signature. Art historians invented the name Master of Flémalle for their maker, later argued over whether he was Robert Campin or a pupil, and the debate continues. The panels show how much of an object's meaning arrives packaged with a name.
column5:
KQ: How does not knowing the maker change what a piece is allowed to mean?

Suggested answer 1: Attribution steers value. Scholars built a whole painter, the Master of Flémalle, from style alone, and prices and prestige moved with each new guess about who he was.

Suggested answer 2: Anonymity can free the viewing. Without a biography to lean on, the panels get read through their own choices of light and gesture, so attention returns to the object.
column6: "Robert Campin." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Campin.
column7: Claim card 1: Attribution steers value. | Claim card 2: Anonymity can free the viewing. | Fact card: Three panels in Frankfurt, painted around 1430 in the early Netherlandish style, carry no signature. | Materials: 2 Pigment, 1 Charcoal

column1: Hawaiian Mele Wax Cylinders, Honolulu, 1904
column2: The Unwritten Room: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: In the early 1900s, collectors recorded Hawaiian chants and songs, mele, onto wax cylinders, some now held by the Bishop Museum in Honolulu. The recordings captured voices from a generation born before annexation. Fragile and playable only a few times, the cylinders raise the archivist's dilemma: each playback both shares and wears away the record.
column5:
KQ: Can a recording preserve a tradition, or only one performance of it?

Suggested answer 1: A cylinder freezes a single voice on a single afternoon, so archives hold moments rather than traditions, and living practice keeps the tradition or nothing does.

Suggested answer 2: A frozen moment can still seed a revival. Hawaiian musicians and scholars return to early recordings to recover styles the missionary era suppressed, so the moment serves the living chain.
column6: "Phonograph Cylinder." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phonograph_cylinder. | "Bernice Pauahi Bishop Museum." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bishop_Museum.

column1: HOLC Redlining Map of Detroit, 1939
column2: The Unasked Room: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: The Home Owners' Loan Corporation drew color-graded security maps of American cities in the 1930s, marking mostly Black neighborhoods in red as hazardous for lending. Banks and federal agencies steered money accordingly for decades. Historians and economists use the surviving maps to trace how a bureaucratic drawing shaped generations of wealth and segregation.
column5:
KQ: How can a map create the facts it claims merely to describe?

Suggested answer 1: The map graded neighborhoods for loan risk, lenders followed the grades, investment fled the red zones, and decline followed, so the description manufactured its own evidence.

Suggested answer 2: A map also leaves a trail. Researchers now overlay the 1939 grades on present-day income, health, and heat maps, so the same object which caused harm supplies the proof of it.

column1: Bonshō of Myōshin-ji, Cast 698 CE, Kyoto
column2: The Unnamed Room: locked chamber treasure (needs its keys)
column4: The bronze temple bell at Myōshin-ji in Kyoto carries a cast date of 698 CE, making it Japan's oldest dated bonshō. Struck from outside with a wooden beam, such bells mark time, gather monks, and close the year with 108 tolls. Acousticians have studied its long, layered decay, and monks have simply listened to it for thirteen centuries.
column5:
KQ: What kinds of knowledge live in a sound rather than a sentence?

Suggested answer 1: A temple bell teaches timing, attention, and shared pause without stating anything, so communities can hold procedural and felt knowledge in objects language never fully translates.

Suggested answer 2: The inscription on the bell still speaks in words, dating it to 698, so even the least verbal artifact tends to travel with text, and the two kinds of knowing arrive together.
column6: "Bonshō." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bonsh%C5%8D. | "Myōshin-ji." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/My%C5%8Dshin-ji.

column1: The Diamond Sutra, Printed Scroll, Dunhuang, 868 CE
column2: The Great Library: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: A colophon dates this printed Buddhist scroll to 11 May 868, the oldest dated printed book known. Wang Jie commissioned it 'for universal free distribution' in honour of his parents. Aurel Stein removed it from the Mogao caves in 1907, and the British Library holds it today.
column5:
KQ: Who decides which knowledge should be free for everyone?

Suggested answer 1: The donor paid for printing so anyone could read the sutra. Free copies spread the teaching far beyond one temple.

Suggested answer 2: Free access still follows a choice. One patron picked this text over all others, so a gift can also steer belief.
column6: "Diamond Sutra." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diamond_Sutra. | "Diamond Sutra." Encyclopaedia Britannica, n.d., www.britannica.com/topic/Diamond-Sutra.
column7: Claim card 1: The donor paid for printing so anyone could read the sutra. | Claim card 2: Free access still follows a choice. | Fact card: A colophon dates this printed Buddhist scroll to 11 May 868, the oldest dated printed book known. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Resin

column1: A Dictionary of the English Language, Samuel Johnson, 1755
column2: The Tower of Tongues: hidden. Hint: Shelved behind the folio atlases in the second alcove of the tongue tower.
column4: Samuel Johnson published this dictionary in London in 1755 after nine years of writing. He defined about 40,000 words and supported them with literary quotations. Readers treated it as the authority on English for more than a century.
column5:
KQ: Who decides which words belong in a language?

Suggested answer 1: A single editor drew the line here. Johnson chose the entries and wrote witty definitions, so the standard carried one man's taste.

Suggested answer 2: Usage outvotes any editor. Speakers kept inventing words he excluded, so the language moved on while the book stood still.
column6: "A Dictionary of the English Language." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_the_English_Language. | "Samuel Johnson." Encyclopaedia Britannica, n.d., www.britannica.com/biography/Samuel-Johnson.
column7: Claim card 1: A single editor drew the line here. | Claim card 2: Usage outvotes any editor. | Fact card: Samuel Johnson published this dictionary in London in 1755 after nine years of writing. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Resin

column1: The Vinland Map, Yale Unveiling, 1965
column2: The Great Library: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Yale University revealed this map in 1965 as a claimed medieval chart showing Norse America. Ink studies from the 1970s onward found modern pigment, and Yale declared the map a fake in 2021. Belief in it lasted half a century because the story fit hopes about Viking voyages.
column5:
KQ: How far can laboratory tests settle a dispute between historians?

Suggested answer 1: Chemistry gave a hard answer here. The ink held a modern titanium compound, so the parchment could not carry a medieval drawing.

Suggested answer 2: Tests close one question and open another. Scientists proved forgery, yet historians still ask who drew it and why experts believed.
column6: "Vinland Map." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vinland_Map.
column7: Claim card 1: Chemistry gave a hard answer here. | Claim card 2: Tests close one question and open another. | Fact card: Yale University revealed this map in 1965 as a claimed medieval chart showing Norse America. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Charcoal

column1: Oxford English Dictionary, Part I, A to Ant, 1884
column2: The Tower of Tongues: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: James Murray edited the first part of the Oxford English Dictionary, published in 1884 and covering A to Ant. Thousands of volunteer readers sent quotation slips, among them William Chester Minor, a patient held at Broadmoor asylum. The full first edition took until 1928 to complete.
column5:
KQ: To what extent can a dictionary describe a language without ruling it?

Suggested answer 1: The editors aimed to record, not command. Volunteer readers mailed quotation slips, so definitions followed printed use.

Suggested answer 2: Selection still rules quietly. Editors chose which slips counted, so the record carries their judgment about good sources.

column1: Gutenberg Bible, 42-Line Edition, Mainz, c. 1455
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Great Library), about 150 lumens
column4: Johannes Gutenberg printed about 180 copies of this Latin Bible in Mainz around 1455 using movable metal type. Copies matched each other page for page, a new fact in European book history. Within fifty years, presses ran in over two hundred European towns.
column5:
KQ: How far does faster copying shift control over a message?

Suggested answer 1: Print multiplied the text beyond any censor's reach. Identical Bibles left one press, so no single scribe or bishop could edit them all.

Suggested answer 2: The press first served the old authority. Gutenberg printed the church's own book, so new tools often begin by strengthening the earlier voice.

column1: Shakespeare's First Folio, London, 1623
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Great Library), about 120 lumens
column4: John Heminges and Henry Condell, actors in Shakespeare's company, assembled this collection in 1623, seven years after his death. It preserved eighteen plays with no earlier printed text, including Macbeth and The Tempest. About 235 copies survive today.
column5:
KQ: What do we lose when a performance becomes a printed page?

Suggested answer 1: The page saves the words and drops the event. Eighteen plays had no other copy, so print rescued them from silence.

Suggested answer 2: A script is a seed, not the play. Each staging remakes the meaning, so the Folio fixes far less than it seems.
column6: "First Folio." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Folio. | "First Folio." Encyclopaedia Britannica, n.d., www.britannica.com/topic/First-Folio.

column1: The Voynich Manuscript, c. 1420
column2: The Great Library: first-win reward of the Cipher of the Stacks trial
column4: This illustrated manuscript surfaced in 1912 when the book dealer Wilfrid Voynich bought it near Rome. Radiocarbon dating places its vellum in the early 1400s. Nobody has read its looping script, despite attempts by wartime code breakers and modern computers.
column5:
KQ: Under what circumstances should experts admit a text may never be read?

Suggested answer 1: Effort has limits. A century of code breakers failed, so a careful reader holds the question open instead of forcing an answer.

Suggested answer 2: Unread does not mean unreadable. Linear B waited three thousand years for decipherment, so new tools or one insight could still unlock it.
column6: "Voynich manuscript." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voynich_manuscript.
column7: Claim card 1: Effort has limits. | Claim card 2: Unread does not mean unreadable. | Fact card: This illustrated manuscript surfaced in 1912 when the book dealer Wilfrid Voynich bought it near Rome. | Materials: 2 Glass, 1 Cord

column1: Leonardo da Vinci's Codex Leicester, c. 1510
column2: Bottega District: given by Ser Piero the copyist after an exchange
column4: Leonardo filled these 72 pages around 1508 to 1510 with studies of water, rock layers, and moonlight, all in mirror script. He explained fossil shells on mountains long before geology named the process. Bill Gates bought the codex in 1994 for 30.8 million dollars.
column5:
KQ: How do private notebooks change our picture of discovery?

Suggested answer 1: Notebooks show thinking in motion. Leonardo asks why the sky is blue and argues with himself, so science looks like drafts, not verdicts.

Suggested answer 2: Private pages can also bury knowledge. He wrote in mirror script and never published, so his insights moved nobody for centuries.
column6: "Codex Leicester." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Codex_Leicester.
column7: Claim card 1: Notebooks show thinking in motion. | Claim card 2: Private pages can also bury knowledge. | Fact card: Leonardo filled these 72 pages around 1508 to 1510 with studies of water, rock layers, and moonlight, all in mirror script. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Charcoal

column1: Fermat's Margin Note in Diophantus' Arithmetica, 1637
column2: The Great Library: hidden. Hint: Slipped inside the Arithmetica on the margin-readers' lectern.
column4: Around 1637, Pierre de Fermat wrote in his copy of Diophantus, claiming a marvellous proof too large for the margin. The note reached print in 1670 through his son's edition. Andrew Wiles finally proved the theorem in 1995.
column5:
KQ: Does a claim without its proof count as knowledge?

Suggested answer 1: In mathematics, no proof means no knowledge. The margin held a boast, and the field waited 358 years for the missing reason.

Suggested answer 2: The claim still guided knowers. Generations tested it and trusted it, so an unproven statement can organize a whole field's search.
column6: "Fermat's Last Theorem." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermat%27s_Last_Theorem. | "Pierre de Fermat." Encyclopaedia Britannica, n.d., www.britannica.com/biography/Pierre-de-Fermat.
column7: Claim card 1: In mathematics, no proof means no knowledge. | Claim card 2: The claim still guided knowers. | Fact card: Around 1637, Pierre de Fermat wrote in his copy of Diophantus, claiming a marvellous proof too large for the margin. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Charcoal

column1: Beethoven's Conversation Books, Vienna, 1818-1827
column2: The Hall of Mirrors: given by Anton the secretary after an exchange
column4: Deaf from around 1818, Beethoven asked visitors to write their questions in notebooks and answered aloud. About 139 books survive. His secretary Anton Schindler destroyed some and forged entries in others to inflate his own role, a fraud confirmed by researchers in the 1970s.
column5:
KQ: How should historians treat evidence curated by someone with an agenda?

Suggested answer 1: Every source needs a suspect list. Schindler added fake entries after Beethoven died, so scholars now weigh each page against outside records.

Suggested answer 2: Tainted evidence still speaks. Even forged lines show what Schindler wanted the world to believe, so the lies become data about him.
column6: "Anton Schindler." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anton_Schindler. | "Ludwig van Beethoven." Encyclopaedia Britannica, n.d., www.britannica.com/biography/Ludwig-van-Beethoven.
column7: Claim card 1: Every source needs a suspect list. | Claim card 2: Tainted evidence still speaks. | Fact card: Deaf from around 1818, Beethoven asked visitors to write their questions in notebooks and answered aloud. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Cord

column1: The Flood Tablet, Gilgamesh XI, Nineveh, 7th Century BCE
column2: The Great Library: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: George Smith read this tablet at the British Museum in 1872 and, by report, jumped up in excitement: it held a flood story older than the Bible's. Scribes copied it for King Ashurbanipal's library at Nineveh in the 7th century BCE. The find opened public debate about where scripture's stories began.
column5:
KQ: To what extent can an older text change the meaning of a newer one?

Suggested answer 1: Age rearranges the family tree of stories. This flood tale predates Genesis, so readers began asking who borrowed from whom.

Suggested answer 2: Meaning does not travel by date alone. Each community reads its own flood story for its own lessons, so the older text changed history more than faith.
column6: "Epic of Gilgamesh." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epic_of_Gilgamesh. | "Epic of Gilgamesh." Encyclopaedia Britannica, n.d., www.britannica.com/topic/Epic-of-Gilgamesh.
column7: Claim card 1: Age rearranges the family tree of stories. | Claim card 2: Meaning does not travel by date alone. | Fact card: George Smith read this tablet at the British Museum in 1872 and, by report, jumped up in excitement: it held a flood story older than the Bible's. | Materials: 2 Stone, 1 Charcoal

column1: Popol Vuh, Father Ximenez Manuscript, c. 1701
column2: Maya Lowlands: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: K'iche' Maya authors wrote this creation narrative in the Latin alphabet in the 1550s, soon after Spanish rule began. Friar Francisco Ximenez copied the only surviving version around 1701, with a Spanish translation beside the K'iche'. The text tells of the Hero Twins and of people shaped from maize.
column5:
KQ: What happens to knowledge when survivors must store it in the colonizer's alphabet?

Suggested answer 1: The alphabet saved the story. K'iche' authors wrote their creation account in Latin letters, so the narrative outlived the burned hieroglyphic books.

Suggested answer 2: Rescue changed the container. A Spanish friar copied and translated it, so every reader now meets the text through colonial hands.

column1: Information Management: A Proposal, CERN, March 1989
column2: The Ring: first-win reward of the Rung Climb trial
column4: Tim Berners-Lee handed this memo to his CERN supervisor in March 1989, proposing linked documents across computers. Mike Sendall wrote 'vague, but exciting' on the cover and let him try. The prototype became the World Wide Web, released to all in 1993.
column5:
KQ: Under what circumstances should an institution back an idea it cannot yet evaluate?

Suggested answer 1: Big claims need small tests. Berners-Lee's manager let him build a prototype on the side, so a cheap trial answered what review could not.

Suggested answer 2: Evaluation frames can blind us. The memo fit no existing category at CERN, so the sorting system, not the idea, produced the doubt.

column1: The Florentine Codex, Mexico City, 1577
column2: Maya Lowlands: given by Fray Bernardino after an exchange
column4: Franciscan friar Bernardino de Sahagun and Nahua scholars completed this twelve-book study of Aztec life in 1577, written in parallel Nahuatl and Spanish columns with over 2,000 images. Nahua elders gave testimony and trained scribes recorded it. The Spanish crown then banned such research, and the volumes ended up in Florence.
column5:
KQ: Who deserves the name of author when many hands build the record?

Suggested answer 1: Credit follows the title page. Sahagun signed as author, so Nahua scholars like Antonio Valeriano stayed invisible for centuries.

Suggested answer 2: The text itself reveals the team. Whole sections run in Nahuatl with Indigenous illustrations, so careful readers can restore the missing names.

column1: The Negro Motorist Green Book, 1940 Edition
column2: Harlem: hidden. Hint: Tucked in the glovebox of the parked sedan by the print shop.
column4: Victor Hugo Green, a Harlem postal carrier, published this guide yearly from 1936 to 1966. It listed hotels, filling stations, and private homes where Black motorists could stop safely under segregation. Green wrote of a future day when his guide could go out of print.
column5:
KQ: What does a travel guide reveal about who moves safely through a country?

Suggested answer 1: A guidebook can map danger instead of sights. Green listed which motels served Black travellers, so its pages recorded a divided nation.

Suggested answer 2: The same pages carried dignity. Families planned holidays with confidence, so knowledge turned a hostile road into a passable one.

column1: Tarikh al-Sudan Chronicle, Timbuktu, c. 1655
column2: Sankoré Courtyard: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Abd al-Rahman al-Sadi completed this Arabic chronicle of the Songhai empire and Timbuktu around 1655. European scholars long claimed sub-Saharan Africa lacked written history, and this book helped bury the claim. Thousands of similar manuscripts survive in Timbuktu's family libraries.
column5:
KQ: How does a written chronicle change what later generations can claim about a city?

Suggested answer 1: Documents anchor memory. Al-Sadi recorded scholars, trade, and kings, so Timbuktu's golden age rests on named pages, not rumour.

Suggested answer 2: One chronicle is one window. The author wrote from his city's scholar class, so herders, women, and rivals appear only at the edges.

column1: Abdel Kader Haidara's Manuscript Footlockers, Timbuktu, 2012
column2: Sankoré Courtyard: given by Abdel Kader the librarian after an exchange
column4: When armed groups occupied Timbuktu in 2012, the librarian Abdel Kader Haidara organized couriers to move about 350,000 manuscripts to Bamako in metal trunks, by road and by river. Occupiers burned over 4,000 manuscripts left behind in 2013. Digitization projects now copy the rescued pages.
column5:
KQ: Who bears responsibility for saving knowledge when a state cannot?

Suggested answer 1: Custodians act first. Families had guarded these pages for centuries, so librarians, not armies, smuggled them to safety.

Suggested answer 2: Rescue by individuals stays fragile. One checkpoint search could have ended it, so lasting protection still needs institutions and law.

column1: Kitab al-Jabr of al-Khwarizmi, Baghdad, c. 820
column2: House of Wisdom: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi wrote this treatise on completion and balancing in Baghdad around 820. The word algebra comes from al-jabr in its title, and algorithm comes from his name. He included step-by-step examples for inheritance shares and trade.
column5:
KQ: What makes a method more valuable than a single answer?

Suggested answer 1: A method solves whole families of problems. Al-Khwarizmi showed steps anyone could repeat, so algebra outlived every equation in the book.

Suggested answer 2: Methods hide their origins. He solved problems in words and diagrams, without symbols, so today's x and y can distort what he explained.

column1: Ibn al-Haytham's Book of Optics, Cairo, 1021
column2: House of Wisdom: given by Rashid the lens grinder after an exchange
column4: Writing near Cairo around 1021, Ibn al-Haytham argued light travels into the eye and proved key points with camera obscura experiments. He urged readers to test every claim, including his own. Latin translations carried his method into European science.
column5:
KQ: What role does deliberate testing play in trusting what we see?

Suggested answer 1: Sight alone misleads. Ibn al-Haytham showed vision needs light entering the eye and tested his claims with dark chambers, so seeing became an experiment.

Suggested answer 2: Testing rests on sight in the end. Every reading of every instrument reaches us through the senses, so we refine our trust, we never escape it.

column1: The Fihrist of al-Nadim, Baghdad, 987
column2: House of Wisdom: hidden. Hint: Behind the bookseller's curtain, lowest drawer of the catalogue chest.
column4: The Baghdad bookseller Ibn al-Nadim compiled this index of all books known to him in 987. It records thousands of titles, many now lost, covering law, poetry, science, and alchemy. Historians use it to glimpse the shelves of a vanished city.
column5:
KQ: What does a catalogue of every known book say about a culture's idea of knowledge?

Suggested answer 1: A list draws a map of learning. Al-Nadim included Greek, Persian, and Indian authors, so tenth-century Baghdad counted foreign thought as its own inheritance.

Suggested answer 2: A catalogue also fixes borders. He sorted books into his ten categories, so later readers met knowledge pre-arranged by one bookseller's mind.

column1: Yongle Dadian Encyclopedia, Ming China, 1408
column2: The Grand Historian's Study: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: About 2,000 scholars completed this encyclopedia for the Yongle Emperor in 1408, filling more than 11,000 hand-copied volumes. No press ever printed it, and fewer than 400 volumes survive today. Scholars have recovered many lost texts from its quotations.
column5:
KQ: Who benefits when a state gathers all knowledge into one set of books?

Suggested answer 1: The throne gains a monument and a filter. The Yongle Emperor ordered the compilation, so inclusion followed imperial approval.

Suggested answer 2: Later knowers gain a lifeboat. Lost books survive only as passages quoted inside it, so an emperor's showpiece became a rescue archive.

column1: Marie Curie's Laboratory Notebooks, Paris, 1897-1902
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Ring), about 90 lumens
column4: Marie Curie recorded her radium and polonium experiments in these notebooks between 1897 and 1902. The pages remain radioactive, so the French national library stores them in lead-lined boxes and asks readers to sign a waiver. Curie won Nobel Prizes in physics in 1903 and chemistry in 1911.
column5:
KQ: To what extent can a measurement stay separate from the person measuring?

Suggested answer 1: Numbers aim at independence. Curie logged masses and readings anyone could repeat, so radium's claim rested on records, not reputation.

Suggested answer 2: The record keeps the body in it. Radioactive traces from her own hands still mark the pages, so the observer never fully leaves the data.

column1: The Muqaddimah of Ibn Khaldun, 1377
column2: House of Wisdom: first-win reward of the Assumption Sweep trial
column4: Ibn Khaldun wrote this introduction to history in 1377, in present-day Algeria. It tells historians to reject reports conflicting with the observed laws of society and to trace how dynasties rise and decay. Many scholars treat it as an early foundation for sociology and source criticism.
column5:
KQ: How can historians test reports of events they cannot witness?

Suggested answer 1: Ibn Khaldun offered a filter: check each report against how societies actually behave. Impossible numbers and flattering tales fail the test.

Suggested answer 2: The filter has its own settings. His rules grew from his experience of dynasties and deserts, so one thinker's laws can misjudge another region's record.

column1: The Einstein-Szilard Letter to Roosevelt, August 2, 1939
column2: The Ring: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Physicist Leo Szilard drafted this letter and Albert Einstein signed it on 2 August 1939. It warned President Roosevelt uranium could yield extremely strong bombs of a new type and urged American action. The letter helped trigger the research effort leading to the Manhattan Project.
column5:
KQ: What responsibility do scientists carry for how leaders use their warnings?

Suggested answer 1: Knowledge of danger demands speech. Szilard saw fission's military path first, so silence would have handed the choice to chance.

Suggested answer 2: A warning starts a machine its authors cannot steer. The letter helped launch a bomb project neither man controlled, and Einstein later regretted signing.

column1: Tabula Rogeriana of al-Idrisi, Palermo, 1154
column2: House of Wisdom: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Muhammad al-Idrisi completed this world map and geography for King Roger II of Sicily in 1154, drawing on interviews with travellers and merchants. South sits at the top, following Islamic mapping practice. It remained among the most accurate world descriptions for centuries.
column5:
KQ: How does the mapmaker's home shape the world the map shows?

Suggested answer 1: Every map has a centre of gravity. Al-Idrisi detailed the lands he knew from travellers, so his world leans toward Islamic trade routes.

Suggested answer 2: Orientation is convention, not error. His map puts south at the top, so the page reminds us every direction on a map is a choice.

column1: Treaty of Tordesillas, Signed Parchment, June 7, 1494
column2: The Forum of Claims: hidden. Hint: In the map room's locked drawer, beneath the folded charts.
column4: Spain and Portugal signed this treaty on 7 June 1494, dividing newly found lands along a meridian west of the Cape Verde islands. No African, Asian, or American nation took part. The line steered Portuguese claims to Brazil in 1500.
column5:
KQ: By what right can a line on a map divide lands the drawers never saw?

Suggested answer 1: The line was authority talking to itself. Spain and Portugal split future discoveries with papal blessing, and nobody from the other continents signed.

Suggested answer 2: Even an arrogant line produces facts. Brazil speaks Portuguese today partly because of this parchment, so a paper claim became a lived border.

column1: Kangnido World Map, Joseon Korea, 1402
column2: Seoul: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Korean scholars produced this world map in 1402, blending Chinese, Islamic, and Korean sources. It shows Africa with open sea below its tip, decades before Portuguese ships rounded the Cape. Korea and China dominate the map's space.
column5:
KQ: What does a map's edge say about the limits of its makers' knowledge?

Suggested answer 1: Edges confess. Europe and Africa shrink to thin shapes at the margin, so the map admits where Korean sources ran out.

Suggested answer 2: Presence beats precision. Africa appears at all, with a rounded southern tip, so Asian scholars clearly gathered knowledge far beyond their own coasts.

column1: Mao Kun Map of Zheng He's Routes, Printed 1628
column2: Kerala Coast: first-win reward of the Boundary Maze trial
column4: This long strip map records the sea routes of Zheng He's fleets, which reached East Africa between 1405 and 1433. Mao Yuanyi printed it in his 1628 military encyclopedia Wubei Zhi. It gives compass bearings and star altitudes for harbours from China to Hormuz.
column5:
KQ: How can sailing directions preserve voyages a state chose to forget?

Suggested answer 1: Practical text outlives policy. The strip chart lists compass bearings and star heights to Africa, so the routes survived after the fleets stopped.

Suggested answer 2: Survival came at one remove. The map reached us inside a 1628 military book, redrawn from lost originals, so we read the voyages through an editor's choices.

column1: Yuktibhasa of Jyesthadeva, Kerala, c. 1530
column2: Kerala Coast: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Jyesthadeva composed this Malayalam text around 1530, setting out proofs for the infinite series of the Kerala school, including series for pi found long before European calculus. Palm-leaf copies preserved the text locally for centuries. Historians now cite it as a landmark of mathematical justification.
column5:
KQ: Why does mathematics demand reasons as well as correct results?

Suggested answer 1: Proof turns a result into knowledge. Jyesthadeva wrote out the reasoning behind infinite series, so readers could check instead of believe.

Suggested answer 2: Reasons travel badly across borders. The proofs stayed in Malayalam palm leaves unknown to Europe, so justification alone gave the ideas no global life.

column1: The Bakhshali Manuscript, Birch Bark, c. 3rd Century CE
column2: Kerala Coast: hidden. Hint: Inside the birch-bark bundle on the lowest shelf of the counting house.
column4: A farmer dug up this birch-bark mathematics text near Bakhshali, in present-day Pakistan, in 1881. It uses a dot as a placeholder zero. Oxford's 2017 radiocarbon tests dated different folios to different centuries, from roughly 224 to 993 CE, and scholars still argue over the split.
column5:
KQ: How certain can we be about the age of a document written in pieces?

Suggested answer 1: Science gave a spread, not a date. Radiocarbon placed the folios centuries apart, so the manuscript is a collection, and each page needs its own claim.

Suggested answer 2: Uncertainty can still move a field. Even the youngest reading keeps a written zero very early, so a fuzzy date can carry a solid conclusion.

column1: Panini's Ashtadhyayi, Sanskrit Grammar, c. 400 BCE
column2: The Tower of Tongues: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Panini composed this grammar of Sanskrit in roughly 4,000 brief rules around the 4th century BCE. The rules apply in ordered steps, and modern linguists compare the system to a formal computer grammar. It fixed the standard for classical Sanskrit for over two thousand years.
column5:
KQ: Can a living language be captured completely by a set of rules?

Suggested answer 1: Panini came astonishingly close. His 4,000 compact rules generate correct Sanskrit forms, so grammar behaved like a machine long before machines.

Suggested answer 2: A rulebook freezes a river. Spoken language kept drifting after him, so his grammar preserved one polished register, not the living whole.

column1: Hunminjeongeum Haerye, Hangul Manual, 1446
column2: Seoul: given by Scholar Shin after an exchange
column4: King Sejong announced the Hangul alphabet in 1443, and this 1446 manual explains the design and purpose of the letters. Their shapes mirror the mouth producing each sound. The book claims a clever man can learn the script in a morning, and a slow one within ten days.
column5:
KQ: What changes in a society when reading no longer belongs to an elite?

Suggested answer 1: Access rewires who may know. Hangul's simple letters let commoners and women write, so new voices entered the written record.

Suggested answer 2: Elites rarely retire quietly. Scholars kept Chinese characters for serious learning long after, so scripts can share a country and still split its status.

column1: Jikji, Metal Movable Type, Cheongju, 1377
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from Seoul), about 140 lumens
column4: Buddhist monks printed Jikji with movable metal type in Cheongju, Korea, in 1377, 78 years before Gutenberg's Bible. A French diplomat acquired the surviving volume around 1900, and the National Library of France keeps it today. UNESCO added Jikji to its Memory of the World register in 2001.
column5:
KQ: Who should hold a treasure when its home and its keeper disagree?

Suggested answer 1: Objects teach where people can reach them. The surviving volume sits in Paris, far from Cheongju, so a French reading room decides who meets Korea's printing history.

Suggested answer 2: Custody is not the same as meaning. Korea built a museum and UNESCO listed the book, so a nation can reclaim the story without holding the paper.

column1: Kaitai Shinsho, New Book of Anatomy, Edo, 1774
column2: Ukiyo District: given by Genpaku the translator after an exchange
column4: Sugita Genpaku and his colleagues watched a dissection in 1771 and saw a Dutch anatomy book match the body better than their Chinese sources. They spent three years translating it with almost no dictionary and published Kaitai Shinsho in 1774. The book pushed Japanese medicine toward European methods within the Dutch learning movement called rangaku.
column5:
KQ: Under what circumstances should a knower abandon an inherited authority?

Suggested answer 1: Direct observation outranks the classics. The Dutch plates matched the body on the table and the Chinese texts did not, so the translators switched authorities.

Suggested answer 2: Switching authority is still trusting. The team could barely read Dutch and copied its errors too, so they traded one borrowed truth for another.

column1: Records of the Grand Historian, Shiji, c. 94 BCE
column2: The Grand Historian's Study: hidden. Hint: Behind the bamboo-slip bundles in the third pigeonhole of the historian's desk.
column4: Sima Qian completed this 130-chapter history of China around 94 BCE, covering two thousand years from the Yellow Emperor to his own era. After offending Emperor Wu, he chose punishment and prison over an honourable death so he could finish the book. Chinese dynastic histories followed his model for two millennia.
column5:
KQ: What price should a knower accept to finish telling the truth?

Suggested answer 1: Sima Qian chose disgrace over silence. He accepted a shaming punishment rather than abandon his father's history, so the record survived at a personal price.

Suggested answer 2: Sacrifice does not certify accuracy. His suffering moves readers to trust him, yet loyalty to the Han court and to his father still shaped what he wrote.

column1: Inscribed Oracle Bone, Anyang, c. 1200 BCE
column2: The Grand Historian's Study: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Shang diviners carved questions on ox bones and turtle shells near Anyang around 1200 BCE, then read heat cracks as answers. Scholars identified the writing in 1899 after inscribed bones appeared in medicine shops. The inscriptions confirmed the Shang kings named in much later histories.
column5:
KQ: How far does the survival of evidence depend on accident?

Suggested answer 1: Chance curated this archive. Farmers sold the bones as dragon-bone medicine until 1899, so a pharmacy accident returned a dynasty to history.

Suggested answer 2: Accident still needs a prepared reader. Wang Yirong recognized writing where buyers saw medicine, so a find counts only when someone can read it.

column1: Veritable Records of the Joseon Dynasty, 1392-1863
column2: The Grand Historian's Study: given by Recorder Han after an exchange
column4: Court historians recorded daily royal affairs across Korea's Joseon dynasty, compiling official annals after each king died. Rules barred a sitting king from reading the drafts, shielding the recorders. The annals fill 1,893 volumes and sit on UNESCO's Memory of the World register.
column5:
KQ: How far does independence from authority protect the objectivity of a record?

Suggested answer 1: Distance defends the record. Reigning kings could not read the drafts, so historians wrote without fear of the throne's edits.

Suggested answer 2: Rules bend under enough pressure. A few kings still peeked or purged historians, so objectivity lived in daily practice, not on paper alone.

column1: Dream Pool Essays of Shen Kuo, Song China, 1088
column2: The Grand Historian's Study: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: The Song dynasty official Shen Kuo compiled these notebooks around 1088, covering canals, astronomy, fossils, and Bi Sheng's movable type. He gave the first clear description of a magnetic compass used for finding direction and noted its needle points slightly off true north. Historians mine the text for early Chinese science.
column5:
KQ: How does a notebook of scattered observations become science?

Suggested answer 1: Careful description invites later theory. Shen Kuo logged the compass needle's slight lean away from true north, so a jotting became a base for navigation.

Suggested answer 2: Scattered notes lack a system. His entries mix fossils, printing, and omens without method, so readers must supply the connecting explanation.

column1: The Histories of Herodotus, c. 430 BCE
column2: The Grand Historian's Study: first-win reward of the Fallacy Volley trial
column4: Herodotus of Halicarnassus completed his account of the Greek-Persian wars around 430 BCE, gathering reports through travel and interviews. He often presents several versions of an event and lets readers judge. Cicero later called him the father of history, and critics answered with father of lies.
column5:
KQ: When does collecting stories become writing history?

Suggested answer 1: Method makes the difference. Herodotus names sources, compares versions, and flags his doubts, so curiosity grew into a discipline.

Suggested answer 2: He also repeats gold-digging ants and flying snakes. Readers call him father of history and father of lies, so the title depends on which pages you weigh.

column1: What Is History? by E. H. Carr, Cambridge, 1961
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Grand Historian's Study), about 60 lumens
column4: E. H. Carr delivered these lectures at Cambridge in 1961 and published them the same year. He attacked the idea of history as a simple pile of facts, describing instead an unending dialogue between historian and evidence. The book remains a standard first reading in historiography.
column5:
KQ: To what extent do historians create the past they describe?

Suggested answer 1: Facts never speak alone. Carr argued historians select which events count, so every history is a conversation between present and past.

Suggested answer 2: Selection is not invention. Surviving documents still limit the claims a careful historian can defend, so creation has borders.

column1: El primer nueva coronica y buen gobierno, Peru, 1615
column2: Cusco of Two Records: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, an Indigenous Andean, finished this illustrated chronicle around 1615 as a letter to King Philip III, documenting Inca history and colonial abuse. The manuscript surfaced in Copenhagen's royal library in 1908. Its drawings now anchor whole fields of Andean history.
column5:
KQ: How can the colonized answer the official record?

Suggested answer 1: Guaman Poma wrote back. His 1,189-page letter to the Spanish king mixes Andean views with nearly 400 drawings, so the archive holds an Indigenous rebuttal.

Suggested answer 2: An unread answer changes little in its own time. The king likely never saw it, and it lay lost until 1908, so counter-records often win only across centuries.

column1: Inca Accounting Khipu, Puruchuco Archive, c. 1500
column2: Cusco of Two Records: hidden. Hint: Coiled inside the storeroom jar, left of the accounting bench.
column4: Inca administrators recorded numbers in knotted cords called khipu, and a set found at Puruchuco near Lima suggests linked levels of accounting around 1500. Spanish rule displaced the practice, and the reading tradition died. Researchers have decoded the number system, while any narrative content stays unread.
column5:
KQ: Is a text still a text if nobody alive can read it?

Suggested answer 1: Reading is a relationship, not a property. The cords held census and tribute data for trained keepers, so the knowledge waits for readers, like any lost script.

Suggested answer 2: Without readers, objects fall silent. We recover numbers from the knots but no names or stories, so part of the medium may stay archaeology forever.

column1: Sejarah Melayu, The Malay Annals, Raffles MS 18, 1612
column2: Kerala Coast: given by The court scribe after an exchange
column4: This Malay court chronicle traces the kings of Melaka, and its 1612 recension is linked to the noble Tun Sri Lanang. It blends verifiable events with royal legend, opening with descent from Alexander the Great. UNESCO lists the Annals in its Memory of the World register.
column5:
KQ: What is a chronicle for, if not accuracy?

Suggested answer 1: Chronicles build legitimacy. The Annals join Melaka's kings to Alexander the Great, so the text's task was authority, and myth served it well.

Suggested answer 2: Purpose does not cancel evidence. Between the legends sit court customs, trade, and titles historians confirm elsewhere, so one book holds both charter and record.

column1: The Requerimiento, Spanish Ultimatum Text, 1513
column2: The Forum of Claims: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Spanish jurists drafted this proclamation in 1513 for use in the Americas. Conquistadors read it aloud in Spanish, sometimes to empty beaches or sleeping villages, declaring Spanish rule and faith before attacking. Critics such as Bartolome de las Casas mocked the practice within its own century.
column5:
KQ: Can an announcement no listener understands ever justify anything?

Suggested answer 1: The document served the speakers, not the hearers. Officials read Spanish legal claims to villages and empty shores, so the ritual manufactured a consent nobody gave.

Suggested answer 2: Even a hollow ritual reveals a conscience. Spain felt it needed legal form before conquest, so the text records an empire arguing with itself about right.

column1: Encyclopedie of Diderot and d'Alembert, Volume I, 1751
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Forum of Claims), about 130 lumens
column4: Denis Diderot and Jean d'Alembert published the first volume of the Encyclopedie in Paris in 1751. The full project reached 28 volumes and over 70,000 articles, mapping trades, sciences, and contested ideas. French authorities revoked its licence in 1759, and printing continued half-underground.
column5:
KQ: Why do authorities fear a book whose aim is to explain everything?

Suggested answer 1: Explanation redistributes authority. Craft secrets and church claims sat in the same alphabet, so ranking by reason replaced ranking by rank.

Suggested answer 2: Fear also advertised the project. Bans and revoked licences made the volumes famous, so censorship helped sell the Enlightenment.

column1: Truth and Reconciliation Commission Report, South Africa, 1998
column2: Truth Commission Hall: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Archbishop Desmond Tutu chaired this commission, which heard over 21,000 victim statements about apartheid-era abuses and published a five-volume report in 1998. Perpetrators could request amnesty in exchange for full disclosure. Many later commissions worldwide adapted the model.
column5:
KQ: Can a nation agree on truth without agreeing on justice?

Suggested answer 1: The commission traded punishment for testimony. Amnesty unlocked confessions victims could finally hear, so the country gained truths courts might never reach.

Suggested answer 2: Some called the trade unjust. Perpetrators left free with their confessions, so the report's truth carries an unpaid bill.

column1: Samizdat Typescript of The Gulag Archipelago, Moscow, 1974
column2: The Forum of Claims: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Soviet authorities banned Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn's account of the labour camp system, first published abroad in 1973. Readers circulated hidden typescripts and photocopies, a practice called samizdat, self-publishing. Possession could end a career or bring arrest, and the author lost his citizenship in 1974.
column5:
KQ: How does knowledge move when publishing one is a crime?

Suggested answer 1: Readers became the press. Typists retyped carbon copies and passed them hand to hand, so distribution itself turned into resistance.

Suggested answer 2: Scarcity shapes reception. A borrowed copy read in one night leaves no time to check claims, so forbidden texts earn belief partly through danger.

column1: Stasi Shredded-File Sacks, Berlin, 1989
column2: Truth Commission Hall: given by Frau Poppe the archivist after an exchange
column4: East Germany's secret police shredded files as the state collapsed in 1989, leaving about 16,000 sacks of torn paper. Citizens occupied the offices to save the archives, and a federal agency has reassembled pages by hand and by computer since the 1990s. Millions have applied to read their own files.
column5:
KQ: What do we owe people whose lives were recorded without consent?

Suggested answer 1: Access repays surveillance. Germany opened the files so citizens could read their own records and name their informers, so transparency became the remedy.

Suggested answer 2: Opening files reopens wounds. A page can reveal a spying spouse or friend, so some argue sealed archives protect the living better than disclosure does.

column1: Anne Frank's Red-Checked Diary, Amsterdam, 1942
column2: The Hall of Mirrors: hidden. Hint: Behind the hinged bookcase in the annex model, second landing.
column4: Anne Frank received this diary for her thirteenth birthday in June 1942 and wrote in hiding until August 1944. She revised her own entries after hearing a radio appeal to preserve wartime diaries. Her father Otto published an edited version in 1947, and fuller critical editions followed decades later.
column5:
KQ: Whose choices shape a diary between the writing and the reading?

Suggested answer 1: Editors stand between us and the page. Anne rewrote entries herself and her father removed private passages, so the published book is already an interpretation.

Suggested answer 2: The voice still holds the centre. Restored editions let readers compare the versions, so we can watch the diary become a book and still hear her.

column1: The Kurukan Fuga Charter, Mande Assembly, c. 1236
column2: Griot Roads: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Tradition holds Sundiata Keita's assembly proclaimed this charter after the battle of Krina around 1236, setting rules for clans, property, and social duties in the Mali empire. Griots transmitted it orally for centuries, and researchers compiled a written version from oral sources in 1998. UNESCO recognized the Manden Charter in 2009.
column5:
KQ: Does a constitution need writing to bind a people?

Suggested answer 1: Memory can carry law. Griots preserved the charter's rules in trained speech for centuries, so the binding lived in people, not paper.

Suggested answer 2: Oral law bends with its speakers. Versions differ between lineages, and the 1998 transcription froze one telling, so memory and writing each trade fidelity for reach.

column1: Sundiata, An Epic of Old Mali, Niane Edition, 1960
column2: Griot Roads: given by Djeli Mamadou after an exchange
column4: The historian D. T. Niane published this French prose version in 1960, based on the telling of the griot Mamadou Kouyate. The epic recounts Sundiata Keita founding the Mali empire in the 13th century. Griot families still perform their own versions across West Africa.
column5:
KQ: What survives and what dies when an oral epic enters print?

Suggested answer 1: Print rescues reach. Niane's French text carried Sundiata into classrooms worldwide, so the hero outgrew his region.

Suggested answer 2: Print removes performance. The fixed page drops music, audience, and the griot's living updates, so readers meet a transcript, not the epic.

column1: Keep Calm and Carry On Poster, London, 1939
column2: The Forum of Claims: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Britain's Ministry of Information printed about 2.45 million copies of this poster in 1939 and held it back for a severe invasion crisis. It never went on official display, and many copies were pulped. A bookshop in Alnwick found one in 2000, and reprints carried the design worldwide.
column5:
KQ: Who controls what a message means once its moment has passed?

Suggested answer 1: Context writes half the message. The poster waited for bombs and stayed in storage, so a 2000 rediscovery let a bookshop, then the internet, decide its meaning.

Suggested answer 2: Design carries intent across decades. The crown and the calm imperative still signal wartime Britain, so the original purpose keeps a grip on every parody.

column1: The Catalan Atlas, Majorca, 1375
column2: The Great Library: hidden. Hint: Rolled in the leather tube above the compass-rose cabinet.
column4: Abraham Cresques, a Jewish mapmaker in Majorca, produced this atlas around 1375 for the French court. It shows Mansa Musa of Mali enthroned with a gold nugget, drawn from trade route reports. The panels blend accurate coastlines with marvels further inland.
column5:
KQ: What can a map tell us about lands its maker never visited?

Suggested answer 1: Maps compile testimony. Cresques drew Mali's king holding a gold nugget from traveller and trade reports, so the sheet stores other people's journeys.

Suggested answer 2: Second-hand gold glitters brightest. Mansa Musa's wealth grew in each retelling, so the image records Europe's appetite along with Africa's riches.

column1: Waldseemuller World Map, Universalis Cosmographia, 1507
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Great Library), about 160 lumens
column4: Martin Waldseemuller printed this twelve-sheet world map in 1507 and named the new continent America, honouring Amerigo Vespucci. He removed the name from later maps, but other mapmakers had adopted it. The only complete surviving copy hangs in the Library of Congress, bought in 2003 for ten million dollars.
column5:
KQ: How does a name on a map become a fact on the ground?

Suggested answer 1: Print gives a christening reach. The map labelled the new continent America after Amerigo Vespucci, and a thousand copies made the name too common to recall.

Suggested answer 2: The mapmaker repented too late. Waldseemuller dropped the name from his later maps, so authority over a word had passed from author to audience.

column1: Mercator's World Map for Navigators, Duisburg, 1569
column2: The Great Library: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Gerardus Mercator published this projection in 1569 to help sailors plot straight compass courses. Its mathematics stretches land areas near the poles and shrinks the tropics by comparison. Wall maps kept the projection long after navigation moved on, feeding debates about distorted world views.
column5:
KQ: Can a map be correct and misleading at the same time?

Suggested answer 1: Correct for its users. Straight compass courses cross Mercator's grid truthfully, so sailors got exactly the accuracy they needed.

Suggested answer 2: Misleading for everyone else. The projection swells Greenland to rival Africa, so classroom walls taught generations a distorted world.

column1: The Peters World Map, Equal-Area Projection, 1974
column2: The Forum of Claims: first-win reward of the Perspective Prism trial
column4: The historian Arno Peters presented this equal-area world map in 1973, arguing Mercator-based maps belittled the Global South. Cartographers pointed to James Gall's earlier identical projection and criticized the stretched shapes. UNESCO and aid organizations adopted it widely, and the dispute reshaped map literacy.
column5:
KQ: Whose interests does a neutral-looking image of the world serve?

Suggested answer 1: Peters called Mercator a colonial relic. His equal-area map restores the tropics' true size, so aid agencies adopted it to re-centre the Global South.

Suggested answer 2: Correction brought new distortion. Shapes stretch until continents drip, and cartographers note older equal-area maps existed, so rival maps sell rival stories.

column1: John Snow's Cholera Map, Broad Street, London, 1854
column2: The Aggregate: hidden. Hint: Pinned behind the Broad Street pump model, lower corner.
column4: The physician John Snow mapped cholera deaths near London's Broad Street pump during the 1854 outbreak. The clustering supported his claim of waterborne spread, against the ruling bad-air theory. Officials removed the pump handle, and epidemiology gained a founding image.
column5:
KQ: How can arranging old facts on a page create new knowledge?

Suggested answer 1: Position was the proof. Deaths clustered around one water pump once dotted on a street plan, so the map argued what the tables could not.

Suggested answer 2: The map convinced with help. Snow already suspected water and questioned households door to door, so the picture sealed a case built by interviews.

column1: We Can Do It! Poster, Westinghouse Plant, 1943
column2: The Forum of Claims: hidden. Hint: Rolled behind the factory lockers in the print rack.
column4: J. Howard Miller designed this poster in 1943 for Westinghouse factories, where it hung for about two weeks to encourage wartime production. Few people saw it during the war, and the Rosie the Riveter label came later. Rediscovered in the 1980s, it became a global emblem of women's strength.
column5:
KQ: Under what circumstances does an image acquire a meaning its makers never planned?

Suggested answer 1: Audiences finish the message. Feminists revived the forgotten poster in the 1980s as an icon of women's strength, so today's meaning is a later gift.

Suggested answer 2: The first purpose still teaches. Westinghouse displayed it for two weeks to steady wartime production, not to liberate anyone, so context checks the myth.

column1: Rebbelib Stick Chart, Marshall Islands, 19th Century
column2: Wayfinder's Ocean: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Marshallese navigators bound palm ribs and cowrie shells into charts modelling how ocean swells bend around atolls. A pilot memorized his own chart before a voyage and sailed without it. Collectors gathered many in the 19th century, and the wave-reading tradition, weakened under colonial rule, continues in revival programs.
column5:
KQ: Can knowledge be stored in an object only its maker can read?

Suggested answer 1: The chart was a memory aid, not a message. Shells marked atolls and curved ribs marked swells for the navigator who bound them, so the medium presumed its author.

Suggested answer 2: Objects outlive private codes. Museum charts now teach wave piloting in general, so even a personal tool can seed public knowledge once experts translate it.

column1: Tupaia's Chart of Pacific Islands, HMS Endeavour, 1769
column2: Wayfinder's Ocean: given by Tupaia's apprentice after an exchange
column4: Tupaia, a Polynesian priest and navigator from Raiatea, drew this chart of about 74 islands with James Cook's crew in 1769. Scholars long called it confused, and recent studies argue it encodes bearings in a Polynesian system. Tupaia also piloted the Endeavour to Rurutu and interpreted in New Zealand.
column5:
KQ: What gets lost when one knowledge system is drawn inside another's format?

Suggested answer 1: Translation bent the content. Tupaia arranged islands by sailing logic while European readers expected fixed distances, so the chart confused its students.

Suggested answer 2: The document still proves mastery. It names dozens of islands across thousands of kilometres, so the paper records a navigation science Europe lacked words for.

column1: Letter from Birmingham Jail, April 16, 1963
column2: The Forum of Claims: given by The courier after an exchange
column4: Martin Luther King Jr. wrote this open letter on 16 April 1963 while jailed for marching in Birmingham, Alabama, beginning on newspaper margins and scraps. It answers white clergymen who had called the protests unwise and defends nonviolent direct action. The letter now anchors courses on civil disobedience.
column5:
KQ: Who decides whether a law deserves obedience?

Suggested answer 1: King answered with criteria. A just law uplifts human personality and binds everyone equally, so citizens can test statutes instead of simply obeying them.

Suggested answer 2: Self-chosen criteria alarm others. The clergymen he answered feared chaos if every conscience picks its laws, so justification must convince beyond the believer.

column1: John Harrison's H4 Sea Watch, London, 1759
column2: Royal Society Court: given by The Board's clerk after an exchange
column4: John Harrison completed this large silver watch in 1759 to solve the longitude problem. It kept time within seconds on a 1761-62 voyage to Jamaica, and Parliament's Board of Longitude required a second trial before releasing rewards in stages. Accurate sea clocks finally let navigators fix east-west position.
column5:
KQ: How many successful tests turn a device into trusted knowledge?

Suggested answer 1: One triumph can be luck. H4 lost only seconds crossing to Jamaica, yet the Board demanded a second voyage, so repetition, not brilliance, bought trust.

Suggested answer 2: Institutions also protect their bets. Astronomers on the Board favoured their rival lunar method, so the demand for more proof mixed rigour with rivalry.

column1: The Nautical Almanac, First Edition, London, 1767
column2: Royal Society Court: hidden. Hint: Under the tide tables at the observatory clerk's desk.
column4: Astronomer Royal Nevil Maskelyne published the first Nautical Almanac in 1767, tabulating the Moon's position so sailors could find longitude. Freelance human computers calculated the tables to fixed procedures with built-in cross-checks. Sailors relied on its successors for two centuries.
column5:
KQ: To what extent can procedures replace individual genius in producing knowledge?

Suggested answer 1: Rules scale better than brilliance. Human computers followed printed instructions and cross-checked each other's sums, so ordinary diligence produced navigation-grade truth.

Suggested answer 2: Procedures inherit their designer. Maskelyne set the methods and tolerances, so one astronomer's judgment hid inside every anonymous table.

column1: Darwin's Notebook B, the 'I think' Tree Sketch, 1837
column2: The Beagle's Wake: hidden. Hint: Wedged between two beetle boxes in the ship-cabin trunk.
column4: Charles Darwin opened Notebook B on the transmutation of species in 1837, the year after the Beagle returned. One page holds a branching diagram of species under the words 'I think'. Cambridge University Library reported two such notebooks missing in 2020, and they returned anonymously in 2022 in a pink gift bag.
column5:
KQ: What does a first sketch reveal about how explanations are born?

Suggested answer 1: Ideas start as doodles with doubts. Darwin drew a branching tree and wrote 'I think' above it, so a world-changing theory began as a hedge.

Suggested answer 2: The sketch was already loaded. Years of Beagle specimens stood behind those branches, so the flash of insight compressed a long apprenticeship.

column1: The Zinoviev Letter, Daily Mail Front Page, October 25, 1924
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Forum of Claims), about 30 lumens
column4: The Daily Mail published this letter on 25 October 1924, presenting it as the Soviet official Grigory Zinoviev instructing British communists, four days before the general election. Labour lost, and historians along with a 1999 Foreign Office review judged the letter a forgery by persons still uncertain. It remains Britain's classic election-eve fake.
column5:
KQ: What role does timing play in the damage a false document can do?

Suggested answer 1: Four days left no room for checking. The letter ran just before the election and denials came after the vote, so the calendar did the forgery's heavy lifting.

Suggested answer 2: Damage needs a ready audience. Readers already feared communist plots, so the fake supplied evidence for a belief waiting to be confirmed.

column1: Dewey Defeats Truman, Chicago Daily Tribune, November 3, 1948
column2: The Forum of Claims: first-win reward of the Assumption Sweep trial
column4: The Chicago Daily Tribune printed this early edition headline on 3 November 1948, declaring Thomas Dewey the winner over President Harry Truman. Polling had stopped weeks early and sampled unevenly, and a strike pushed printing deadlines forward. Truman won and posed laughing with the paper.
column5:
KQ: Under what circumstances does more data bring more confidence but not more truth?

Suggested answer 1: The sample betrayed the sum. Phone polls over-reached wealthier voters and pollsters stopped asking weeks early, so mountains of skewed numbers built one wrong headline.

Suggested answer 2: Deadlines finished the fallacy. A printers' strike forced typesetting before the results, so the paper printed its expectation and called it news.

column1: J'Accuse...! Front Page of L'Aurore, January 13, 1898
column2: The Forum of Claims: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Emile Zola published this open letter to France's president on the front page of L'Aurore on 13 January 1898, accusing the army of framing Alfred Dreyfus, a Jewish officer. Courts convicted Zola of libel and he fled to England, yet the case reopened. France later cleared and reinstated Dreyfus.
column5:
KQ: What risks should a knower take to force hidden evidence into the open?

Suggested answer 1: Zola gambled his freedom on a headline. He invited his own libel trial so suppressed evidence about Dreyfus would enter a courtroom, so risk became a method.

Suggested answer 2: Spectacle cuts both ways. The front page split France into camps as much as it revealed facts, so forcing truth loudly can harden an audience against it.

column1: The Great Moon Hoax, The Sun, New York, August 25, 1835
column2: The Forum of Claims: first-win reward of the Fallacy Volley trial
column4: The New York Sun ran six articles from 25 August 1835 describing bat-winged people and unicorns on the Moon, credited falsely to the astronomer John Herschel. Circulation soared, and rival papers reprinted the series. The Sun never fully retracted it, and the case became a founding study of media hoaxes.
column5:
KQ: Why do audiences believe spectacular claims from ordinary sources?

Suggested answer 1: Borrowed authority does the lifting. The articles cited a famous astronomer and a serious journal, both misused, so trust in science carried the bat-men.

Suggested answer 2: Wanting wonder lowers the bar. Readers enjoyed the story too much to test it, so the appetite for marvels, then and now, feeds the fake.

column1: Domesday Book, Royal Survey of England, 1086
column2: The Forum of Claims: given by The Exchequer clerk after an exchange
column4: William the Conqueror ordered this survey of England in 1085, and clerks completed the main volumes by 1086, recording landholders, livestock, and values across thousands of settlements. Within a century people called it Domesday, the day of judgment, because its entries allowed no appeal. The UK National Archives holds it today.
column5:
KQ: What does a ruler gain by counting everything a country owns?

Suggested answer 1: Counting converts land into control. William's commissioners listed every plough and pig for tax and dispute, so the survey turned conquest into administration.

Suggested answer 2: The count also fixed the counted. Villagers nicknamed it Domesday because its word was final, so a database became a verdict nobody could appeal.

column1: The Donation of Constantine, Exposed by Valla, 1440
column2: The Tower of Tongues: first-win reward of the Claim Forge trial
column4: This document claimed Emperor Constantine granted the papacy rule over the western empire, and popes cited it for centuries. In 1440 Lorenzo Valla proved from its Latin style and anachronisms the text arose centuries after Constantine. His method helped found modern textual criticism.
column5:
KQ: How can the language of a document betray its true age?

Suggested answer 1: Words carry timestamps. Valla showed the Latin used terms unknown in Constantine's century, so grammar convicted the parchment of forgery.

Suggested answer 2: Motive found the method. Valla served a king quarrelling with the pope, so his brilliant philology also had a paymaster, and readers should weigh both.

column1: The Code of Hammurabi Stela, Babylon, c. 1754 BCE
column2: The Forum of Claims: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Hammurabi of Babylon had this basalt stela carved around 1754 BCE with 282 laws on trade, family, and injury. The top shows the god Shamash granting him authority. French archaeologists found it at Susa in 1901, and the Louvre displays it.
column5:
KQ: Why do rulers carve laws in stone instead of keeping them in memory?

Suggested answer 1: Stone makes justice checkable. Anyone could point to the carved penalty for a broken tooth, so written law promised protection from a judge's whim.

Suggested answer 2: Stone also stages authority. The relief shows the sun god handing Hammurabi the rules, so the medium justifies the king along with the laws.

column1: Sequoyah's Cherokee Syllabary Chart, 1821
column2: The Tower of Tongues: first-win reward of the Cipher of the Stacks trial
column4: Sequoyah, a Cherokee silversmith, completed his syllabary in 1821 after about twelve years of solo experiments. Each of its 85 characters records a syllable, and learners gained literacy within weeks. The Cherokee Nation adopted it officially, and the Cherokee Phoenix newspaper printed in it from 1828.
column5:
KQ: Can someone invent a writing system without first being able to read?

Suggested answer 1: Sequoyah did exactly this. He could not read English yet built 85 signs for Cherokee syllables, so invention needed insight into his own language, not schooling in another.

Suggested answer 2: He still borrowed shapes. Letter forms from English printing fill the chart with new sounds attached, so even radical invention recycles available materials.

KQ: What does the care given to copying reveal about a community's values?

Suggested answer 1: Precision was devotion. Carvers produced over 81,000 blocks with almost no errors during an invasion, so accuracy itself became an offering.

Suggested answer 2: The project also served the state. The court sponsored the canon to rally protection for the kingdom, so sacred care and political need shared the chisel.

column2: The Pilgrim Roads: hidden. Hint: Wrapped in silk inside the drum case at the monastery treasury door.
column4: Monks at Abba Garima monastery in Ethiopia preserved these two illuminated Ge'ez gospel books for centuries, with tradition crediting the founding saint. Radiocarbon tests reported around 2010 dated pages to roughly 390-660 CE, placing them among the earliest illuminated Christian manuscripts. The books have never left the monastery.
column5:
KQ: How should belief respond when science redates a sacred object?

Suggested answer 1: The lab strengthened the legend's core. Radiocarbon moved the gospels back near the monastery's founding era, so tradition gained scientific company.

Suggested answer 2: Convergence here sets no rule. The same method has undermined other relics, so communities must decide in advance whether dating counts only when it flatters.
