column2: The Painted Cave: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: Researchers cored ancient bristlecone pines in California's White Mountains from the 1950s. Some trees have lived over 4,000 years. Their rings built a year-by-year record used to check radiocarbon dates.
column5:
KQ: How can a tree hold a calendar more exact than human memory?

Suggested answer 1: Each ring marks one year. Wide and narrow bands log wet and dry seasons, so the core dates events to the year.

Suggested answer 2: Rings can still mislead. A hard year may skip a ring, so readers cross-check many trees to fix the count.

column1: Rachel Carson's 'Silent Spring', First Edition, 1962
column2: Q'ero Highlands: given by Ranger Alma after an exchange
column4: Biologist Rachel Carson published Silent Spring in 1962. The book showed how the pesticide DDT moved through food chains and harmed wildlife. It helped launch the modern environmental movement and a later ban on DDT for farming.
column5:
KQ: Who bears the duty to warn a public about a slow harm?

Suggested answer 1: A writer can force a reckoning. Carson linked pesticides to dying birds, so her book made a hidden harm public.

Suggested answer 2: One book can also overreach. Critics said she pressed the case hard, so readers must weigh her claims against later data.

column2: Q'ero Highlands: first-win reward of the Socratic Serve trial
column4: In 2016 members of the Standing Rock Sioux and allies camped to block the Dakota Access Pipeline near their water source in North Dakota. They said the route threatened sacred land and the Missouri River. Police cleared the camp in early 2017.
column5:
KQ: How far can a protest camp challenge a pipeline backed by law?

Suggested answer 1: A camp can shift the story. Thousands gathered at Standing Rock, so a local fight became a global cause.

Suggested answer 2: Law and money can still win. Crews finished the pipeline in 2017, so protest raised the question yet lost the ground.

column2: Songlines Country: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: In 1963 Yolngu leaders in Arnhem Land sent bark petitions to Australia's Parliament. Painted clan designs framed a typed protest against mining on their land. The petitions became a founding document in the fight for Aboriginal land rights.
column5:
KQ: Can a people prove they own land without a deed on paper?

Suggested answer 1: Old use can ground a claim. The Yolngu painted their law onto bark, so their own record spoke for ancient title.

Suggested answer 2: Foreign courts may not read it. Parliament first set the bark aside, so proof means little without power to hear it.

Suggested answer 1: Marks can jog a set message. A runner read the notches with practiced memory, so wood and mind joined as one.

Suggested answer 1: A model can capture a pattern. Curved sticks map how swells bend around islands, so a navigator learns the ocean's shape.

Suggested answer 2: A model is not the sea itself. Charts stayed on shore as study aids, so skill at sea still came from the body and eye.

column1: Mabo v Queensland Native Title Decision, 1992
column2: Songlines Country: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: In 1992 Australia's High Court decided Mabo v Queensland in favor of Eddie Mabo and the Meriam people. The judges rejected terra nullius, the claim the land had belonged to no one. The case created native title in Australian law.
column5:
KQ: Under what circumstances should a court undo a founding legal myth?

Suggested answer 1: Evidence can topple a false doctrine. Eddie Mabo proved long Meriam land use, so the court struck the idea of empty land.

Suggested answer 2: A ruling can arrive too late for many. Two hundred years of loss came first, so justice on paper met a long, hard past.

Suggested answer 1: Exposure can guard democracy. Snowden showed mass data collection, so citizens learned what leaders hid from them.

Suggested answer 2: Leaks can also endanger. Secret programs may protect people too, so releasing them shifts risk in ways one leaker cannot judge alone.

Suggested answer 1: Watching can rule without force. The design let one guard seem always present, so inmates policed themselves.

Suggested answer 2: The eye needs belief to bite. Prisoners who doubt the watcher may act free, so the design controls minds, not bodies.

Suggested answer 1: A lens records without a stake. It captures the scene as light hits it, so footage can check rival human accounts.

column1: Bertillon Criminal Measurement Card, Paris, 1883
column2: The Aggregate: first-win reward of the Assumption Sweep trial
column4: French officer Alphonse Bertillon built a system of body measurements to identify criminals from 1883. Clerks recorded set lengths on cards with mug shots. Fingerprints later replaced the method after it misidentified people.
column5:
KQ: Can measuring a body reveal the truth of who a person is?

Suggested answer 1: Numbers can fix identity. Bertillon logged ear, arm, and head sizes, so police matched a suspect to a file.

Suggested answer 2: Bodies change and charts fail. Measures drifted and mixed people up, so the system claimed more than it could prove.

column1: Stratton Case Fingerprint Exhibit, London, 1905
column2: Truth Commission Hall: in the open (Field Search or floor pickup)
column4: The 1905 trial of the Stratton brothers in London was the first British murder case won on fingerprint evidence. A print from the crime scene matched one brother. The verdict helped make fingerprints a standard tool in courts.
column5:
KQ: What makes a mark on a surface count as proof in court?

Suggested answer 1: A rare pattern can point to one person. The thumbprint matched a suspect, so the court trusted ridges over eyewitness doubt.

Suggested answer 2: A first use sets a risky rule. Jurors could not check the science, so they leaned on the expert's word, not their own eyes.

column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from Great Zimbabwe), about 40 lumens
column4: Zimbabwe printed a 100 trillion dollar note in 2008 during extreme hyperinflation. Prices rose so fast the note soon bought almost nothing. The country abandoned its currency in 2009.
column5:
KQ: What holds up the value printed on a piece of paper?

Suggested answer 1: Shared belief gives money value. People trade while they trust the note, so faith, not ink, sets its price.

Suggested answer 2: Belief can vanish overnight. Prices doubled in hours in 2008, so the huge number bought less than a loaf of bread.

KQ: Does a public have the right to secrets its leaders keep about a war?

Suggested answer 1: Hidden truth can betray voters. The study showed leaders doubted the war in private, so the leak let citizens judge the record.

Suggested answer 2: States claim secrecy shields lives. Officials said release aided the enemy, so security and openness clash with no clean line.

column4: The Rosetta Stone carries one decree from 196 BCE written in hieroglyphs, Demotic, and Greek. French soldiers found it in Egypt in 1799. Champollion used the Greek to decipher Egyptian hieroglyphs by 1822.
column5:
KQ: How do we crack a script when its last reader died long ago?

Suggested answer 1: A shared text can break a code. The stone gave one decree in three scripts, so Greek unlocked the hieroglyphs beside it.

Suggested answer 2: One key opens only one door. The stone fit a single decree, so scholars still argued over rarer signs for years.

column4: King William I ordered a survey of England in 1086, recorded in the Domesday Book. Officials listed who held each piece of land and its value. The book let the crown tax and rule with a firm grip on facts.

Suggested answer 1: A full count freezes a moment. Clerks logged land, mills, and livestock, so the book still shows England in 1086.

Suggested answer 2: A survey serves its maker. William counted to tax and control, so the record centers wealth, not the poor who tilled the land.

column2: The Aggregate: first-win reward of the Boundary Maze trial
column4: In 1812 Massachusetts redrew a senate district into a long, twisted shape to help the ruling party. A newspaper mocked it as the Gerry-mander after Governor Elbridge Gerry. The word still names maps bent for party gain.

Suggested answer 1: Shaped lines can pick the winner. The 1812 map bent a district like a salamander, so the party chose its voters first.

Suggested answer 2: Voters keep some counter-power. Courts and anger can redraw maps, so a clever line rules only until people fight it.

column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Aggregate), about 50 lumens
column4: Herman Hollerith built a punch-card machine to tally the 1890 United States census. Clerks punched holes for each person's traits, and machines counted them. The method sped the census and seeded the future computer industry.
column5:
KQ: What do we gain and lose when we turn people into punched holes?

Suggested answer 1: Cards can count a nation fast. Machines read the holes in weeks, so the census freed itself from years of hand tallies.

Suggested answer 2: Holes strip away the person. A card keeps age and race, not a life, so speed came at the cost of every story behind it.

column1: WSPU 'Deeds Not Words' Banner, Britain, 1908
column2: The Forum of Claims: first-win reward of the Perspective Prism trial
column4: The Women's Social and Political Union carried banners like Deeds Not Words from 1908 in Britain. The group pushed for votes for women through protest and direct action. Their bold cloth banners became a mark of the suffragette movement.
column5:
KQ: How far can a slogan speak for everyone it claims to include?

Suggested answer 1: A banner can unite many voices. Deeds Not Words gave the movement one shared cry, so scattered women marched as one.

Suggested answer 2: One slogan cannot hold every view. Some wanted calm reform, so the bold words spoke past the women who feared them.
column6: "Women's Social and Political Union." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Women%27s_Social_and_Political_Union.
column7: Claim card 1: A banner can unite many voices. | Claim card 2: One slogan cannot hold every view. | Fact card: The Women's Social and Political Union carried banners like Deeds Not Words from 1908 in Britain. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Cord

column1: The Geneva Conventions, Switzerland, 1949
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from Truth Commission Hall), about 55 lumens
column4: Nations signed the updated Geneva Conventions in 1949 after the Second World War. The treaties set rules for treating prisoners, the wounded, and civilians in war. Most countries on earth have now agreed to them.
column5:
KQ: Can rules make even war a little more humane?

Suggested answer 1: Shared limits can shield the weak. The conventions protect prisoners and the wounded, so law reaches into the chaos of battle.

Suggested answer 2: Rules bend when states choose. Armies break them and escape blame, so the promise holds only where power agrees.
column6: "Geneva Conventions." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geneva_Conventions.
column7: Claim card 1: Shared limits can shield the weak. | Claim card 2: Rules bend when states choose. | Fact card: Nations signed the updated Geneva Conventions in 1949 after the Second World War. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Cord

column1: Peace of Westphalia Treaty, 1648
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Forum of Claims), about 65 lumens
column4: The Peace of Westphalia in 1648 ended the Thirty Years War in Europe. The treaties set the idea that each state runs its own affairs inside its borders. Scholars trace the modern state system to this settlement.
column5:
KQ: What gives a state the right to rule its own land without outside hands?

Suggested answer 1: A shared deal can build order. The treaties ended a long war by fixing borders and rule, so states agreed to leave each other be.

Suggested answer 2: The idea can shield abuse. A ruler can claim no outsider may judge him, so sovereignty guards tyrants along with peace.
column6: "Peace of Westphalia." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia.
column7: Claim card 1: A shared deal can build order. | Claim card 2: The idea can shield abuse. | Fact card: The Peace of Westphalia in 1648 ended the Thirty Years War in Europe. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Cord
column13: Carried: courier route to The Grand Historian's Study

column1: Trail of Tears National Historic Marker, 1838
column2: Truth Commission Hall: hidden. Hint: Under the flat stone at the base of the trail post.
column4: The United States forced the Cherokee and other nations west along the Trail of Tears in 1838. Thousands died from cold, hunger, and disease on the march. Markers and a national trail now trace the route across several states.
column5:
KQ: Whose account should a roadside marker tell?

Suggested answer 1: A marker can name a wrong. Signs now trace the forced march, so the land itself carries the Cherokee story.

Suggested answer 2: A short plaque can soften horror. A few lines cannot hold thousands of deaths, so the marker risks a tidy version of terror.
column6: "Trail of Tears." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trail_of_Tears.
column7: Claim card 1: A marker can name a wrong. | Claim card 2: A short plaque can soften horror. | Fact card: The United States forced the Cherokee and other nations west along the Trail of Tears in 1838. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Resin

column1: Lakota Ghost Dance Shirt, Wounded Knee, 1890
column2: Q'ero Highlands: given by Dancer's Grandson Mato after an exchange
column4: The Ghost Dance movement spread among Plains nations around 1890. Dancers wore special shirts some believed could stop bullets. United States troops killed hundreds of Lakota at Wounded Knee in December 1890.
column5:
KQ: Can belief in an object change what people dare to do?

Suggested answer 1: Faith can steel a people. Dancers believed the shirt turned bullets, so hope pushed them to stand against the army.

Suggested answer 2: Belief cannot bend fact. The shirts did not stop the guns at Wounded Knee, so faith met a hard and deadly limit.
column6: "Ghost Dance." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_Dance.
column7: Claim card 1: Faith can steel a people. | Claim card 2: Belief cannot bend fact. | Fact card: The Ghost Dance movement spread among Plains nations around 1890. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Resin
column13: Carried: courier route to Songlines Country

column1: Ashanti Golden Stool (Sika Dwa Kofi), Ghana
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from Griot Roads), about 90 lumens
column4: The Ashanti of Ghana hold the Golden Stool as the sacred seat of their nation's soul. Tradition says it descended from the sky around 1700. A British demand to sit on it helped spark the War of the Golden Stool in 1900.
column5:
KQ: How can an object hold the soul of a whole nation?

Suggested answer 1: A people can vest identity in one thing. The Ashanti see the stool as their nation's soul, so no one may sit on it.

Suggested answer 2: Outsiders miss the meaning. The British demanded the stool and sparked a war, so they read gold where a people saw spirit.
column6: "Golden Stool." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Golden_Stool.
column7: Claim card 1: A people can vest identity in one thing. | Claim card 2: Outsiders miss the meaning. | Fact card: The Ashanti of Ghana hold the Golden Stool as the sacred seat of their nation's soul. | Materials: 2 Silver, 1 Resin

column1: Griot's Kora and Oral History, West Africa
column2: Griot Roads: given by Griot Fode after an exchange
column4: In West Africa, griots serve as trained oral historians, poets, and musicians. Many play the kora, a long-necked harp of twenty-one strings. Griots keep genealogies and history across generations by memory and song.
column5:
KQ: How reliable is a history kept in memory and song?

Suggested answer 1: Trained memory can hold centuries. Griots pass names and deeds down exactly, so song carries a people's record.

Suggested answer 2: Memory can bend to please. A patron pays the singer, so praise may reshape the past to flatter a ruler.
column6: "Griot." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Griot.
column7: Claim card 1: Trained memory can hold centuries. | Claim card 2: Memory can bend to please. | Fact card: In West Africa, griots serve as trained oral historians, poets, and musicians. | Materials: 2 Wood, 1 Charcoal

column1: Louisiana Voter Literacy Test, 1964
column2: Truth Commission Hall: first-win reward of the Fallacy Volley trial
column4: Some United States states used literacy tests to block Black citizens from voting into the 1960s. A 1964 Louisiana test gave impossible tasks and vague rules. Registrars passed or failed people as they wished until the 1965 Voting Rights Act banned the practice.
column5:
KQ: Can a test be a tool of exclusion while it looks fair?

Suggested answer 1: A neutral form can hide a bias. Officials failed Black voters at will, so a level surface masked a rigged game.

Suggested answer 2: The trick lay in the grading, not the page. Any answer could be marked wrong, so the test proves rules mean little without fair hands.
column6: "Literacy test." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Literacy_test.
column7: Claim card 1: A neutral form can hide a bias. | Claim card 2: The trick lay in the grading, not the page. | Fact card: Some United States states used literacy tests to block Black citizens from voting into the 1960s. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Charcoal

column1: The 9/11 Commission Report, United States, 2004
column2: The Forum of Claims: first-win reward of the Claim Forge trial
column4: The United States set up a commission to study the attacks of 11 September 2001. Its 2004 report traced the plot and the failures that missed it. The book became a rare government document read widely by the public.
column5:
KQ: What makes an official inquiry into a disaster convincing?

Suggested answer 1: A wide inquiry can build trust. The commission read millions of pages and heard many voices, so its account earned broad belief.

Suggested answer 2: An official report can still miss or hide. Panels answer to power, so critics probe the gaps a state may prefer to leave.
column6: "9/11 Commission Report." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/9/11_Commission_Report.
column7: Claim card 1: A wide inquiry can build trust. | Claim card 2: An official report can still miss or hide. | Fact card: The United States set up a commission to study the attacks of 11 September 2001. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Cord

column1: Zapruder Film Frame 313, Dallas, 1963
column2: The Hall of Mirrors: hidden. Hint: Spooled inside the film can marked home movies, top shelf.
column4: Abraham Zapruder filmed the killing of President John F. Kennedy in Dallas on 22 November 1963. His short home movie captured the shooting in color. Investigators and doubters have studied the frames ever since.
column5:
KQ: Why can one film of an event still spark endless dispute?

Suggested answer 1: Footage fixes a moment for all. The frames caught the killing in daylight, so everyone argues from the same pictures.

Suggested answer 2: Images do not explain themselves. The film shows motion, not cause, so viewers read plots or chance into the very same frames.
column6: "Zapruder film." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zapruder_film.
column7: Claim card 1: Footage fixes a moment for all. | Claim card 2: Images do not explain themselves. | Fact card: Abraham Zapruder filmed the killing of President John F. | Materials: 2 Glass, 1 Cord

column1: Nazi Racial Classification Chart, Germany, 1935
column2: The Hall of Mirrors: given by Archivist Ilse after an exchange
column4: Nazi Germany's Nuremberg Laws of 1935 sorted people by so-called racial blood. Charts showed who counted as Jewish by ancestry. The classification had no basis in biology and served persecution.
column5:
KQ: What harm follows when a state dresses hatred as science?

Suggested answer 1: False science can license cruelty. The chart sorted people by blood, so pseudo-fact gave murder a lab coat.

Suggested answer 2: Naming a lie as science can also expose it. Later scholars showed the categories held no biology, so the chart now warns how data can be faked.
column6: "Nuremberg Laws." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws.
column7: Claim card 1: False science can license cruelty. | Claim card 2: Naming a lie as science can also expose it. | Fact card: Nazi Germany's Nuremberg Laws of 1935 sorted people by so-called racial blood. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Resin

column1: Kirk Bloodsworth DNA Exoneration File, 1993
column2: Truth Commission Hall: given by Lawyer Deshawn after an exchange
column4: A Maryland court sentenced Kirk Bloodsworth to death for murder in 1985. DNA testing proved his innocence, and he went free in 1993. He was the first American on death row freed by DNA evidence.
column5:
KQ: How far can new evidence overturn a court's certainty?

Suggested answer 1: Fresh tests can free the wronged. DNA cleared Bloodsworth after years on death row, so science corrected a jury's sure verdict.

Suggested answer 2: Doubt should come before the cell. The first trial felt certain too, so the case warns us confidence is not proof.
column6: "Kirk Bloodsworth." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kirk_Bloodsworth.
column7: Claim card 1: Fresh tests can free the wronged. | Claim card 2: Doubt should come before the cell. | Fact card: A Maryland court sentenced Kirk Bloodsworth to death for murder in 1985. | Materials: 2 Glass, 1 Cord

column1: The Vinland Map, Alleged Medieval Map, Exposed 1974
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Great Library), about 45 lumens
column4: The Vinland Map seemed to show Norse knowledge of America before Columbus. Yale published it in 1965 as medieval. Chemical tests later found modern ink, and in 2021 Yale declared the map a 20th-century forgery.
column5:
KQ: How do we decide a famous object is a fake?

Suggested answer 1: Chemistry can catch a lie. Tests found modern ink in the lines, so science dated the map long after its claimed age.

Suggested answer 2: Doubt must still stay open. Experts argued for decades first, so proof of a fake came slowly, not at first glance.
column6: "Vinland Map." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vinland_Map.
column7: Claim card 1: Chemistry can catch a lie. | Claim card 2: Doubt must still stay open. | Fact card: The Vinland Map seemed to show Norse knowledge of America before Columbus. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Charcoal

column1: Piltdown Man Skull Fragments, England, 1912 Hoax
column2: The Beagle's Wake: hidden. Hint: Boxed in cotton wool inside the second museum drawer.
column4: In 1912 Charles Dawson presented skull bones as a missing link between apes and humans, found at Piltdown in England. Scientists accepted it for decades. In 1953 tests showed a human skull joined to an orangutan jaw, a deliberate hoax.
column5:
KQ: Why can a forgery fool experts for years?

Suggested answer 1: A fake can fit a hope. Piltdown matched what scholars wished to find, so bias let a hoax pass as a missing link.

Suggested answer 2: Method can still win in the end. New dating exposed the fraud in 1953, so science corrects itself, if slowly.
column6: "Piltdown Man." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piltdown_Man.
column7: Claim card 1: A fake can fit a hope. | Claim card 2: Method can still win in the end. | Fact card: In 1912 Charles Dawson presented skull bones as a missing link between apes and humans, found at Piltdown in England. | Materials: 2 Bone, 1 Charcoal

column1: Zong Massacre Court Records, England, 1783
column2: Truth Commission Hall: hidden. Hint: Bound in the ledger marked shipping losses, lower archive.
column4: In 1781 the crew of the slave ship Zong threw over 130 enslaved Africans into the sea. The 1783 court case treated the deaths as a claim for lost cargo. The trial exposed the slave trade's cruelty and fed the abolition movement.
column5:
KQ: Can a court paper reveal a crime it never named as murder?

Suggested answer 1: Cold ledgers can expose horror. The case argued over lost cargo, so the record shows enslaved people counted as goods.

Suggested answer 2: The document hides as much as it shows. It speaks of insurance, not murder, so the true crime hides behind money words.
column6: "Zong massacre." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zong_massacre.
column7: Claim card 1: Cold ledgers can expose horror. | Claim card 2: The document hides as much as it shows. | Fact card: In 1781 the crew of the slave ship Zong threw over 130 enslaved Africans into the sea. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Charcoal

column1: Codex Mendoza, Aztec and Spanish, c. 1541
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from Maya Lowlands), about 80 lumens
column4: The Codex Mendoza is an Aztec manuscript made about 1541 under Spanish rule. Native artists painted tribute lists and daily life, and Spanish writers added notes. The book mixes Aztec knowledge with Spanish framing.
column5:
KQ: Whose knowledge speaks when two cultures make one book?

Suggested answer 1: A shared book can hold two voices. Aztec artists drew the pages and Spanish scribes glossed them, so both sides shaped the record.

Suggested answer 2: One side still held the pen last. Spanish patrons ordered the book, so the Aztec view arrives filtered through the conqueror.
column6: "Codex Mendoza." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Codex_Mendoza.
column7: Claim card 1: A shared book can hold two voices. | Claim card 2: One side still held the pen last. | Fact card: The Codex Mendoza is an Aztec manuscript made about 1541 under Spanish rule. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Charcoal

column1: Enola Gay Smithsonian Exhibit Label, 1995
column2: Truth Commission Hall: first-win reward of the Memory of the Hall trial
column4: The Smithsonian planned a 1995 exhibit of the Enola Gay, the plane that dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima. Veterans and others fought over how to describe the mission. The museum cut the display to the plane with a short, plain label.
column5:
KQ: How should a museum display a machine that killed thousands?

Suggested answer 1: A label can invite hard thought. A full account would show the dead below, so the exhibit could teach the cost behind the plane.

Suggested answer 2: Nations guard their heroes' story. Veterans forced a plainer label, so the museum showed the bomber with little of the harm.
column6: "Enola Gay." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enola_Gay.
column7: Claim card 1: A label can invite hard thought. | Claim card 2: Nations guard their heroes' story. | Fact card: The Smithsonian planned a 1995 exhibit of the Enola Gay, the plane that dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima. | Materials: 2 Wood, 1 Charcoal

column1: Paris Agreement Adoption Gavel, COP21, 2015
column2: The Exchange stall (delivered from The Aggregate), about 60 lumens
column4: Nearly two hundred nations adopted the Paris Agreement on climate change in December 2015. The French host banged a green leaf-shaped gavel to seal the deal. Countries set their own emission targets with no strict enforcement.
column5:
KQ: Can a promise without hard penalties still change the world?

Suggested answer 1: A shared goal can steer nations. Almost every country signed in 2015, so the deal set one direction for climate action.

Suggested answer 2: A pledge with no teeth can drift. Nations set their own targets, so the gavel marked hope more than a binding law.
column6: "Paris Agreement." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paris_Agreement.
column7: Claim card 1: A shared goal can steer nations. | Claim card 2: A pledge with no teeth can drift. | Fact card: Nearly two hundred nations adopted the Paris Agreement on climate change in December 2015. | Materials: 2 Wood, 1 Cord

column1: The Nuremberg Code on Human Experiments, 1947
column2: Truth Commission Hall: hidden. Hint: Inside the sealed evidence box beneath the witness stand.
column4: Judges wrote the Nuremberg Code in 1947 after trials of Nazi doctors who ran cruel experiments. It set rules for research on people, starting with informed consent. The code shaped modern medical ethics worldwide.
column5:
KQ: What limits should bind the search for medical knowledge?

Suggested answer 1: Consent must guard the subject. The code demands willing, informed volunteers, so no cure excuses forced harm.

Suggested answer 2: Rules can lag behind abuse. Studies still broke the code for years, so a written limit needs watching to hold.
column6: "Nuremberg Code." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuremberg_Code.
column7: Claim card 1: Consent must guard the subject. | Claim card 2: Rules can lag behind abuse. | Fact card: Judges wrote the Nuremberg Code in 1947 after trials of Nazi doctors who ran cruel experiments. | Materials: 2 Cloth, 1 Cord

column1: The Voynich Manuscript, Undeciphered, c. 1420
column2: The Great Library: first-win reward of the Cipher of the Stacks trial
column4: The Voynich Manuscript is a book of about 240 pages in an unknown script, carbon-dated to around 1420. It shows strange plants, stars, and figures. No scholar has decoded it, and some suspect a hoax.
column5:
KQ: Can a text hold knowledge if no one alive can read it?

Suggested answer 1: A script may still guard meaning. Its plants and charts suggest an actual subject, so knowledge may wait behind the code.

Suggested answer 2: Unread marks may hold nothing. The book could be a clever hoax, so we cannot call it knowledge until we crack it.
column6: "Voynich manuscript." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, n.d., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voynich_manuscript.
column7: Claim card 1: A script may still guard meaning. | Claim card 2: Unread marks may hold nothing. | Fact card: The Voynich Manuscript is a book of about 240 pages in an unknown script, carbon-dated to around 1420. | Materials: 2 Paper, 1 Cord
